message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A retrospective study in Nature recently reported improved overall survival (OS) when COVID-19 mRNA vaccination was administered around initiation of immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) therapy. Whether this association depends on vaccination timing relative to ICI treatment remains unclear.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We conducted a single-centre retrospective cohort study of patients receiving palliative-intent ICI therapy at a UK tertiary cancer centre (October 2014-December 2025). Two vaccination exposure definitions were evaluated: (1) vaccination within 100 days of the first ICI cycle (initial window); and (2) vaccination from 100 days before the first ICI cycle to 100 days after the final ICI cycle (extended window). OS was analysed using Kaplan-Meier methods and Cox models relative to unvaccinated patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Among 2109 patients, 515 (24.4%) received ≥1 COVID-19 vaccine dose. Under the initial window, mRNA vaccination was associated with a longer OS in the all-tumours cohort only (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.58-0.99;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.04). Under the extended window, mRNA vaccination was associated with longer OS in the all-tumours cohort (HR 0.58; 95% CI 0.46-0.75;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    <0.0001), including melanoma (HR 0.35; 95% CI 0.18-0.69;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.002) and kidney cancer (HR 0.47; 95% CI 0.28-0.79;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.004), but not NSCLC. In an era-restricted analysis limited to patients receiving ICI therapy from 2020 onwards, the all-tumours association persisted (HR 0.76; 95% CI 0.59-0.98;
                    <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                    =0.04) with no significant tumour-specific associations.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 mRNA vaccination was associated with improved OS, with magnitude and tumour specificity dependent on vaccination exposure definition. Prospective studies are required to assess causality and tumour-specific effects.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Association of mRNA COVID-19 vaccination with overall survival in patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors: a retrospective cohort analysis
